perturbation.original_text: Unless we agree otherwise, you must open your franchise for business no later than nine (9) months from the Effective Date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless we agree otherwise, you should aim to open your franchise for business around nine (9) months from the Effective Date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'must open' to 'should aim to open' weakens the obligation, and 'no later than' to 'around' introduces significant ambiguity about the deadline, turning a hard deadline into an aspirational goal.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Regulations, Title 16, Section 310(c)
perturbation.law_citation: California Code of Regulations, Title 16, Section 310(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: In California, franchise agreements must have clear and definite terms. By weakening the language around the opening date, and making it ambiguous, the franchise contract may not fulfill its purpose as a guide for operation, contradicting what this law is trying to accomplish.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - California Code of Regulations This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity into a critical term (the opening date), weakening the obligation. The original text stated the franchise 'must open... no later than nine (9) months,' establishing a clear deadline. The revised text says the franchisee 'should aim to open... around nine (9) months,' replacing a definite obligation with an aspirational one and an ambiguous timeframe. The Law Explanation states that California franchise agreements must have clear and definite terms. By weakening the language around the opening date, and making it ambiguous, the modified text contradicts this principle, aligning with the 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: You agree to use in the development and operation of the Franchise only those brands, types, and/or models of equipment, furniture, fixtures, furnishings, and signs we have approved.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree to consider using in the development and operation of the Franchise generally those brands, types, and/or models of equipment, furniture, fixtures, furnishings, and signs we have suggested.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a strict requirement for using only approved items. By changing 'agree to use' to 'agree to consider using' and 'approved' to 'suggested,' the obligation becomes discretionary and significantly less enforceable. Adding 'generally' creates more vagueness.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute §80C.14 Subd. 3 explicitly addresses permissible and impermissible provisions in franchise agreements and the degree of control a franchise agreement can enforce. Modifying the text reduces the franchisor’s control over brand standards, and making it suggestive rather than strict and required might cause issues with quality control, which could violate this Minnesota Statute, impacting the franchisee. Also, the suggested equipment, furnishings, and signs are not clearly determined by the Franchisor, thereby providing ambiguity on what should be installed in the Franchise.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 80C.14 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes COMMERCIAL REGULATIONS Chapter 80C Section 80C.14 80C.13 80C.144 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 80C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 80C.14 Version List 80C.14 UNFAIR PRACTICES. § Subdivision 1. Prohibition. No person, whether by means of a term or condition of a franchise or otherwise, shall engage in any unfair or inequitable practice in contravention of such rules as the commissioner may adopt defining as to franchises the words "unfair and inequitable." For the purpose of rules defining the words "unfair and inequitable," the commissioner may specifically recognize classifications of franchises including but not limited to the classifications of motor vehicle fuel franchises, motor vehicle franchises, hardware franchises, and franchises which require that the franchisee make an initial, unfinanced investment in excess of $200,000. A violation of this section is enjoinable by a court of competent jurisdiction. Irreparable harm to the franchisee will be presumed if there is a violation of this section by a person who is required to register under section 80C.02 , but who fails to do so. A temporary injunction may be granted under this section without requiring the posting of any bond or security. A bond or security is required if a temporary restraining order is granted. § Subd. 2. Acts constituting. All franchise contracts or agreements, other than those classifications of franchises specifically recognized by the commissioner under subdivision 1, and any other device or practice of a franchisor must conform to subdivisions 3 and 4. perturbation.justification: The changed text transforms a strict requirement ('agree to use') into a suggestion ('agree to consider using'), thus weakening the franchisor's control over brand standards. The 'Law Explanation' states that reducing the franchisor's control over brand standards might cause issues with quality control, potentially violating Minnesota Statute §80C.14 Subd. 3. The original contract requires franchisees to use equipment approved by the franchisor, whereas the change makes this discretionary. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 80C.14 UNFAIR PRACTICES. § Subdivision 1. Prohibition. No person, whether by means of a term or condition of a franchise or otherwise, shall engage in any unfair or inequitable practice in contravention of such rules as the commissioner may adopt defining as to franchises the words 'unfair and inequitable.' By making the equipment usage discretionary, there's a possibility of 'unfair and inequitable practice.'
